elements.sent-2411-002: The standard LMM analysis tools for longitudinal data then apply.

elements.sent-2411-006: The missing-data problem has a long history in Statistics (e.g., Afifi and Elashoff 1966, Hartley and Hocking 1971) that featured methodology and computational developments (e.g., Little and Rubin 2014).
elements.sent-2411-007: A standard framework for handling missing data, called missing-data mechanisms (MDM), was set up by Rubin (1976) and followed extensively in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-009: The data are missing completely at random (MCAR) if the probability distribution of the missing does not depend on either the observed or missing values in $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-010: Let $Y_{\rm obs}$ and $Y_{\rm mis}$ denote the observed and missing parts of $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-011: The data are missing at random (MAR) if the probability distribution of the missing depends only on $Y_{\rm obs}$; otherwise, the data are missing not at random (MNAR).
elements.sent-2411-012: Of course, MCAR is a stronger assumption than MAR, but even the latter is usually unverifiable, in practice.
elements.sent-2411-013: The MDM have led to the development of multiple imputation (MI) methods, which apply under the MCAR or MAR assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-014: For the most part, a MI procedure consists of the following steps: (i) impute the missing values with values randomly drawn from some distributions to generate complete cases data set; (ii) perform the same analysis on each of the imputed complete data sets; and (iii) combine the results of the analysis based on different imputed data sets in some way.
elements.sent-2411-015: A version of MI called MICE (multiple imputation via chained equations) has been implemented in R (Van Buuren and Groothuis-Oudshoon 2011).
elements.sent-2411-016: In MICE, the imputation in step (i) is done in a way similar to the Gibbs sampler (e.g., Liu 2004).
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite the profound impact that MI has had in the ``missing-data world'', it may be wondered if there is something conceptually simpler.
elements.sent-2411-018: To see this, let us first consider a simpler case of longitudinal data, in which there are missing covariates but no missing responses.
elements.sent-2411-019: Linear mixed models (LMM) are widely used for longitudinal data since the seminal paper of Laird and Ware (1982).
elements.sent-2411-020: See, for example, Diggle {\it et al. (2002), Jiang and Nguyen (2021).}
elements.sent-2411-021: A standard LMM can be expressed as \begin{equation}y=X\beta+Z\alpha+\epsilon,\label{eq:LMM_eq}\end{equation} where $y$ is the vector of responses, $X$ is the matrix of covariates, $\beta$ is the vector of fixed effects, $Z$ is a known matrix corresponding to $\alpha$, the vector of random effects, and $\epsilon$ is the vector of errors.
elements.sent-2411-022: A defining feature of LMM is the random effects.
elements.sent-2411-023: This actually gives us some idea on how to deal with the missing covariates---we are going to treat them as random effects as well, after suitable centralizations.
elements.sent-2411-024: Note that the LMM already had random effects to start with.
elements.sent-2411-025: At the end of the day, one still has a LMM, albeit a different one.
elements.sent-2411-026: A big difference is that, this time, there are no missing values under the new LMM.
elements.sent-2411-027: An immediate question is whether one gains anything with this random-effects approach.. Intuitively, it should.
elements.sent-2411-028: To see this, let us consider an extreme case, in which the covariate vector is $p$-dimensional, but for every response, there is at least one component of the covariates that is missing.
elements.sent-2411-029: In other words, in the standard LMM equaton, (\ref{eq:LMM_eq ), there is a missing $x$ in every line of the equation.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Clearly, one gets nothing from the LMM analysis based on complete data only.
elements.sent-2411-031: On the other hand, with the random-effects approach, one can, at least, get something; oftentimes (but not all the time), doing something is better than doing nothing.
elements.sent-2411-032: Of course, this is a rather extreme case; in real-life, one may not be so ``bad luck''.
elements.sent-2411-033: But, at the very least, it suggests that the random-effects idea is worth exploring.
elements.sent-2411-034: The next question is what to do when there are missing responses as well.

elements.sent-2411-049: Will this make a difference? Our main finding in this paper is that the random-effects approach, that is, CCE gains over CDOE.

elements.sent-2411-052: For such reasons, we recommend CCE over CCPE.

elements.section-2411-006: Real-data example: Wellness assessment of home-care workers
elements.sent-2411-218: The data considered in this analysis is part of data collected in a daily study conducted by Dr. Ryan Olson and his colleagues at Oregon Health and Science University to measure home-care worker's exposure levels to demanding tasks, and also to understand how work exposure might relate to daily physical symptoms, wellness, and lifestyle behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-219: Twenty-three home-care workers participated in the study, who were predominantly females (21).
elements.sent-2411-220: The participants were 56\% minority, including African American (5), Asian (2), Native American (2), Hispanic/Latino (2), and other (2).
elements.sent-2411-221: The participants worked roughly 30 hours/week, and most (17) regularly performed clients lifting or transferring tasks.
elements.sent-2411-222: After undertaking a baseline demographic and psychosocial survey, the participants completed up to 16 days of daily self-monitoring using the safety task assessment tool developed by the researchers.
elements.sent-2411-223: It was noted that the participants might meet different clients on those days.
elements.sent-2411-224: The work task survey focuses on measuring time and frequency exposures to demanding tasks, and was completed by the worker during the natural breaks at work.
elements.sent-2411-225: The end-of-day survey focuses on recording physical, behavior, and psychosocial symptoms that could be affected by the work task exposures, and was completed in the evening between dinner and bedtime.
elements.sent-2411-226: The analysis here focus on one of the end-of-day survey variables, namely, the Daily Occupational Fatigue (DOF).
elements.sent-2411-227: Twelve other variables are used as predictors, including Time Wheelchair (TW; time spent moving the client around while the client is on wheelchair), Bathing/Toileting Time (BTT; time spent helping the client bathing and using the toilet), Dressing Time (DT; time spent on changing the client's clothes), Transfer Time (TT; time spent completing the transfers), House Clean Time (HCT; time spent cleaning the client's house), Heavy Object Count (HOC; number of heavy objects moved; $>$35 lbs/object), Heavy Object Time (HOT; time spent on moving the heavy objects), Sleep Lag (SL), Pain Lag (PL), Daily Perceived Stress (DPS), Daily Bad Diet Sum (DBDS), and Non-lagged Pain (NLP).
elements.sent-2411-228: The data set involves a large number of missing records, including missing covariates and missing responses.
elements.sent-2411-229: The following LMM is considered in analyzing the data: \begin{eqnarray}y_{ij} = x_{ij}'\beta+v_{i}+e_{ij},\end{eqnarray} $i=1,\dots,m, j=1,\dots,n_{i}$, where $i$ represents the worker and $j$ the study day; $m$ is the number of home-care workers; $n_{i}$ is the number of study days for worker $i$; $x_{ij}$ is the vector of predictive variables as described above, and $\beta$ is a vector of unknown regression coefficients (the fixed effects), including an intercept.
elements.sent-2411-230: Here $v_{i}$ is a worker-specific random effect, and $e_{ij}$ is an additional error.
elements.sent-2411-231: It is assumed that the $v_{i}$'s and $e_{ij}$'s are independent with $v_{i}\sim N(0,\sigma_{v ^{2 )$ and $e_{ij}\sim N(0,\sigma_{e ^{2 )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-232: Table \ref{real_data presents the values of parameter estimates, with the standard errors (SE) in the parentheses, for four different estimates, CDOE, CCE, CCPE and MICE.}
elements.sent-2411-233: CDOE, CCE and CCPE are mostly similar in terms of point estimates, but the MICE estimates appear to be quite different, especially for the intercept.
elements.sent-2411-234: Unlike in the simulation study, here we do not know the true values of these parameters.
elements.sent-2411-235: Nevertheless, it can be seen that the SEs of CCE are uniformly smaller than the SEs of CDOE, and mostly smaller than the SEs of MICE.
elements.sent-2411-236: This seems to be consistent with our simulation results, which suggest that CCE is more efficient than CDOE, and more efficient than MICE for the parameters corresponding to covariates with missing values.
elements.sent-2411-237: It should be noted that the reduced SEs do make differences, in some cases, in terms of the statistical significance.
elements.sent-2411-238: For example, at the 5\% level of significance, the coefficients associated with variables HCT (time spent cleaning the client's house) and SL (Sleep Lag) are significant under the CCE analysis, but not insignificant under the CDOE analysis.
elements.sent-2411-239: The (additional) significance results for the two variables make sense by the common sense as well.
elements.sent-2411-240: One ``surprising'' observation is that the SEs of CCPE are much smaller than the SEs of all of the other estimates.
elements.sent-2411-241: This seems to be inconsistent to our simulation results in Section 4.2, which suggest that CCE and CCPE are almost the same in terms of estimation efficiency; it is also contradicting to our theoretical explanation (see Section 5.2), which suggests that CCPE is not superior than the CCE.
elements.sent-2411-242: But the SE of CCPE is misleading.
elements.sent-2411-243: Note that, in our simulation study, we reported the (simulated) SD (standard deviation), not the SE (standard error), which means an estimated SD.
elements.sent-2411-244: Unlike in the simulated SD in the simulation study, the SE can be a poor estimate of the SD, if something is missing, and the computer does not know it.
elements.sent-2411-245: This seems to be the case here.
elements.sent-2411-246: Recall the CCPE is based on replacing the missing $y_{\rm m}$ by $\hat{y _{\rm m}$, the EBP of $y_{\rm m}$ based on $y_{\rm o}$ and $X$ (see Section 3).}
elements.sent-2411-247: But the computer cannot tell the difference between the two, and treats $\hat{y _{\rm m}$ as observed.}
elements.sent-2411-248: This serious ``misunderstanding'' leads to the fake SEs associated with CCPE, which are significantly smaller than the SEs of CCE (and the other estimates).
elements.sent-2411-249: A take-home message is that CCE is recommended over CCPE, as the latter can produce misleading standard errors, which can impact the inferential results, including false statistical significance and invalid lengths of confidence intervals.
elements.sent-2411-250: The proposed random effects approach to missing values is a natural way of handling missing values in the LMM analysis, because there are already random effects in the model anyway.
elements.sent-2411-251: We showed that, as long as a response is observed, the missing covariates can be mended into a LMM with no missing covariates (the CCE approach), based on which parameter estimation can be done more efficiently compared to the complete-records-only analysis.
elements.sent-2411-252: On the other hand, we showed that there is nothing one could do for the missing responses, even if the latter are replaced by their empirically best predictor based on the observed data (the CCPE approach).
elements.sent-2411-253: In fact, we have explored another approach, in which the missing responses are substituted by bootstrapped data sampled parametrically based on parameters estimated by the observed data.
elements.sent-2411-254: Once again, our simulation results showed no gain over the CCE.
elements.sent-2411-255: This raises a question about whether CCE is indeed the best one can do.
elements.sent-2411-256: We believe it is as long as one is considering the LMM, which is a model conditional on the covariates, $X$, and all the inferential measures, especially the variance, is conditional on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-257: To see this, recall the notation $y_{\rm o}$, $y_{\rm m}$, $X_{\rm o}$ and $X_{\rm m}$ (see Section 3).
elements.sent-2411-258: The CCE is based on completing $X_{\rm o}$, and fitting the LMM (\ref{eq:sub_LMM_o ).}
elements.sent-2411-259: On the other hand, $y_{\rm m}$ is missing.
elements.sent-2411-260: But what about $X_{\rm m}$? There may still be observed components in $X_{\rm m}$, which could provide additional information.
elements.sent-2411-261: Our take on this is that, as long as estimation of $\beta$ is concerned, the useful information should be regarding the relationship between $X$ and $y$.
elements.sent-2411-262: If the $y$ part is missing, the $X$ part is not relevant even if it is observed.
elements.sent-2411-263: However, (the observed part of) $X_{\rm m}$ can still provide additional information if a marginal model is considered, in which both $X$ and $y$ are considered random observations, and the inferential measures, such as the variance, is unconditional, that is, not conditional on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-264: Finally, the random-effects approach is a ``missing at random (MAR)'' scenario (although not necessarily ``missing completely at random'').
elements.sent-2411-265: Note that existing imputation-based methods, such as MICE, are also based on the MAR framework.. {\bf Acknowledgements.}

elements.sent-2411-269: The method converts a GLMM with missing covariates to another GLMM without missing covariates.
elements.sent-2411-270: The standard GLMM analysis tools for longitudinal data then apply.
elements.sent-2411-271: The method applies, in particular, to the cases of linear mixed models and logistic regression.
elements.sent-2411-272: Theoretical justification of the method is given, and explained, for the patterns observed in the simulation studies.
elements.sent-2411-273: Two real-data examples from healthcare studies are discussed.. \vskip5pt\sl Key Words.
elements.sent-2411-274: In this regard, a standard framework for handling the missing data, called missing-data mechanisms (MDM; Rubin 1976), is highly influential.
elements.sent-2411-275: The data are missing completely at random (MCAR) if the probability distribution of the missing does not depend on either the observed or the missing values in $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-276: For the most part, a MI procedure consists of the following steps: (i) impute the missing values with values randomly drawn from some distributions to generate several complete cases data sets; (ii) perform the same analysis on each of the imputed complete data sets; and (iii) combine the results of the analysis based on different imputed data sets in some way.
elements.sent-2411-277: Despite the impact of MI on the ``missing-data world'', it may be wondered if there is something conceptually simpler.
elements.sent-2411-278: Generalized linear mixed models (GLMM) have been widely used for longitudinal data in health and medical studies since the seminal paper of Laird and Ware (1982).
elements.sent-2411-279: Suppose that, conditional on vectors of subject-specific random effects, $\alpha_{i =(\alpha_{ik )_{1\leq k\leq d}$, $1\leq i\leq m$, responses $y_{it}$, $1\leq i\leq m, t\in T_{i}$ are independent with conditional probability density function (pdf), or probability mass function (pmf), given by \begin{equation}f(y_{it}|\alpha)=\exp\left[\left(\frac{a_{it}}{\phi}\right) \{y_{it}\xi_{it}-b(\xi_{it})\}+c\left(y_{it},\frac{\phi}{a_{it}} \right)\right],\label{eq:GLMM}\end{equation} where $b(\cdot)$ and $c(\cdot,\cdot)$ are functions associated with the exponential family (McCullagh and Nelder 1989, \S{2 ), $\phi$ is a dispersion parameter which is sometimes known, $a_{it}$ is a weight such that $a_{it =1$ for un-grouped data; $a_{it =l_{it}$ for grouped data when the group average is considered as response, and $l_{it}$ is the group size; and $a_{it =l_{it ^{-1}$ when the sum of individual responses in the group is considered.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-280: Furthermore, $\xi_{it}$ is associated with a linear predictor, \begin{equation}\eta_{it}=x_{it}'\beta+z_{it}'\alpha_{i},\label{eq:GLMM_pred}\end{equation} through a link function $g(\cdot)$, that is, $g(\xi_{it )=\eta_{it}$, or $\xi_{it =h(\eta_{it )$, where $h=g^{-1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-281: Here $x_{it}$ and $z_{it}$ are known vectors, and $\beta$ is a vector of unknown fixed effects.
elements.sent-2411-282: In the case of a canonical link, we have $\xi_{it =\eta_{it}$.}
elements.sent-2411-283: Finally, suppose that $\alpha_{1 ,\dots, \alpha_{m}$ are independent with pdf $f_{\theta (\cdot)$, where $\theta$ is a vector of variance components.}}
elements.sent-2411-285: As noted, in some cases, such as binomial and Poisson distributions, $\phi$ is known, so $\psi$ represents the vector of all unknown parameters.
elements.sent-2411-286: Two special cases of the GLMM, which are widely used in practice, are the linear mixed models (LMM), in which case the exponential family is normal and $g(u)=u$, and logistic regression, in which the exponential family is binomial, $g(p)={\rm logit (p)=\log\{p/(1-p)\}$, and there is no random effects [i.e., the term $z_{it '\alpha_{i}$ is dropped in (\ref{eq:GLMM_pred )].}}}
elements.sent-2411-287: A defining feature of GLMM is the random effects.
elements.sent-2411-288: This actually gives us some idea on how to deal with the missing covariates.
elements.sent-2411-289: Namely, we are going to treat the missing covariates as random effects as well, after suitable centralizations.
elements.sent-2411-290: Note that the GLMM already has random effects to start with.
elements.sent-2411-291: So, at the end of the day, one still has a GLMM, albeit a different one.
elements.sent-2411-292: A big difference is that, this time, there are no missing values under the new GLMM.
elements.sent-2411-293: In other words, on the right side of (\ref{eq:GLMM_pred ), there is a missing $x$ for every $i,t$.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Clearly, the GLMM analysis based solely on complete data yields nothing.
elements.sent-2411-295: On the other hand, the random-effects approach provides an opportunity to make some progress, which is often (though not always) better than taking no action at all.

elements.sent-2411-496: End-stage renal disease (ESRD) is a major health problem that has led to a significant number of death in the U.S.

elements.sent-2411-498: Existing approaches to reduce the mortality rates include early and accurate diagnosis, managing diabetes and high blood pressure, and dialysis.
elements.sent-2411-499: These strategies require accessibility to quality health care, which many U.S.. citizens are lacking.
elements.sent-2411-500: On March 23, 2010, President Barack Obama signed the Affordable Care Act (ACA; also known as the Obama Care) into law, which was designed to make affordable health insurance available to more people and to expand Medicaid to cover all adults with income below 138\% of the federal poverty level.
elements.sent-2411-501: It is hypothesized that better health care access will decrease the mortality rate due to ESRD.
elements.sent-2411-502: To test this hypothesis, we examined data from the United States Renal Data System (USRDS) collected in 2008-2010 (before the ACA) and compared it to data collected in 2011-2013 (after the ACA) to see if there was a reduction in mortality due to ESRD.
elements.sent-2411-503: We also examined the impact, if any, of the risk factors of diabetes status, race, age, sex, and other comorbidities such as hematocrit level and hemoglobin level on this relationship.
elements.sent-2411-504: The subjects involved were patients with ESRD.
elements.sent-2411-505: Our analysis is based on a logistic regression model.
elements.sent-2411-506: The outcome variable is a binary indicator of death.
elements.sent-2411-507: The predictors (or independent variables) include {\it cohort , a group indicator for the before (0) or after (1) ACA; {\it diabetes , an indicator variable on whether the patient had diabetes; {\it hcrit\_Max , a continuous variable of the maximum hematocrit level from 3-year annual reports for each cohort; {\it hgb\_Max , another continuous variable of the maximum hemoglobin level from 3-year annual reports for each cohort; {\it SEXCODE , an indicator variable for male; {\it distinct\_code\_count , the total number of unique comorbidities; {\it age\_at\_esrd , the age when a patient developed ESRD; {\it RACECODE , a multi-categorial variable with seven categories--Native American (NA), Asian (AS), Black (BLK), White (served as the reference level for creating 6-dummy variables), Unknown (UKN), Hispanic (HIS), and Others; and dialysis modality, categorized into seven categories--hemodialysis (HD) Only served as the reference level, HD to Peritoneal (PD) Late Switch, HD to PD Switch, Multi Switch, PD to HD Late Switch, PD to HD Switch, and PD Only.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-522: According to the results of our simulation study (see Section 4.2), CCE is more efficient for variables with a larger percentage of missing values while MICE is just the opposite, more efficient for variables with no missing values.
elements.sent-2411-523: This seems to be consistent with the findings of this real-data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-524: The proposed random effects approach to missing data is a natural way of handling missing values in the GLMM analysis, because there are already random effects in the model anyway.
elements.sent-2411-525: We showed that, as long as a response is observed, the missing covariates can be mended into a GLMM with no missing covariates (the CCE approach), based on which parameter estimation can be done more efficiently compared to the complete-records-only analysis.
elements.sent-2411-526: This raises a question on whether CCE is indeed the best one can do.
elements.sent-2411-527: We believe it is as long as the analysis is based on a GLMM, which is a model conditional on the covariates, $X$, and all the inferential measures, especially the variance, are also conditional on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-528: Recall the notation $y_{\rm o}$, $y_{\rm m}$, $X_{\rm o}$ and $X_{\rm m}$ (see Section 3).
elements.sent-2411-529: The CCE is based on completing $X_{\rm o}$, and fitting the LMM, (\ref{eq:sub_LMM_o ).}
elements.sent-2411-530: On the other hand, $y_{\rm m}$ is missing, but what about $X_{\rm m}$? There may still be observed components in $X_{\rm m}$, which could provide additional information.
elements.sent-2411-531: Our take on this is that, as long as the estimation of $\beta$ is concerned, the useful information should be regarding the relationship between $X$ and $y$.
elements.sent-2411-532: However, (the observed part of) $X_{\rm m}$ can still provide additional information if a marginal model is considered, in which both $X$ and $y$ are considered random observations, and the inferential measures, such as the variance, is unconditional, taking into account the variation in $X$.
elements.sent-2411-533: The proposed random-effects approach is a ``missing at random (MAR)'' scenario (although not necessarily ``missing completely at random'').
elements.sent-2411-534: Note that existing imputation-based methods, such as MICE, are also based on the MAR framework.
elements.sent-2411-535: Our empirical studies suggest the advantage of the CCE over MICE is more significant under an LMM than under a logistic regression model.
elements.sent-2411-536: We conjecture that the same is also generally true for a GLMM that is not an LMM, and there is an explanation for this.
elements.sent-2411-537: It is known (e.g., Jiang and Nguyen 2009) that, compared to LMM, GLMM is more sensitive to violation of the distributional assumption about the random effects.
elements.sent-2411-538: In CCE, additional random effects are introduced to incorporate the missing values.
elements.sent-2411-539: Such random effects may not satisfy the assumed distributional assumption.
elements.sent-2411-540: A LMM is more robust to such a violation of the distributional assumption than a GLMM; such robustness may affect the efficiency of the CCE.
elements.sent-2411-541: As for the comparison between MICE and CCE in the case of GLMM, our empirical results suggest that MICE is relatively more efficient for estimating coefficients of the covariates that have no or small percentages of missing values, while CCE is more efficient for estimating coefficients of the covariates that have large percentages of missing values.
elements.sent-2411-542: In a way, the latter is more important as the covariates with large percentages of missingness are the ones who are more in need for help.
elements.sent-2411-543: Finally, as illustrated by our real-data example, the CCPE method can produce false statistical significance.

elements.sent-2411-545: Note that, in this case, the term ``computer'' refers to the software package used to perform the statistical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-546: There is a difference between a software package and an artificial intelligence (AI) tool in that the former may be fooled in some ways but the latter is not supposed be, because an AI tool is to work like a human being, who knows what is going on.
